title: Cardiac Arrest — Adult ALS
oneLiner: Loss of cardiac output requiring immediate CPR, defibrillation, and ALS algorithm per Resuscitation Council UK.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Unrecognised reversible cause (4Hs/4Ts):** Missed tension pneumothorax → look for tracheal deviation + distended neck veins + absent breath sounds → needle decompression before rhythm analysis.
- **Prolonged “no flow” time:** Every minute without CPR reduces survival by 7–10% → high-quality, uninterrupted chest compressions are the single most effective intervention; defibrillation alone is useless without perfusion.
- **Failure to identify shockable rhythm:** VF/pVT must be shocked immediately – each minute delay reduces defibrillation success by ~10%. Check pad placement and rhythm after pause <10 seconds.
sections.Presentation:
- **Unresponsive** (no response to voice or pain)
- **Not breathing normally** (agonal gasps count as not normal)
- **No signs of life** (no movement, no coughing, no pulse – but pulse check is unreliable; trust clinical gestalt)
- **Rhythm on monitor:** VF, pulseless VT, asystole, or PEA (any organised rhythm with no pulse)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Shockable: VF / pVT** → immediate defibrillation; treat ischaemia, electrolyte disturbance, drugs (e.g. cocaine, tricyclics)
- **Non-shockable: Asystole / PEA** → adrenaline early; investigate 4Hs/4Ts
    - Hypoxia
    - Hypovolaemia (haemorrhage)
    - Hypo-/hyperkalaemia (check ABG)
    - Hypothermia
    - Tension pneumothorax
    - Tamponade (cardiac)
    - Toxins (overdose)
    - Thrombosis (pulmonary / coronary)
- **Splitter:** Rhythm on monitor – shockable or non-shockable bifurcates the entire management pathway.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - Defibrillator pads + monitor – confirm rhythm
    - Capnography (ETCO₂) – rising waveform marks ROSC; <10 mmHg indicates poor CPR quality
    - Ultrasound (if available and you are trained) – assess for tamponade, pneumothorax, LV contractility; use during <10-second pauses **never** interrupt chest compressions for scanning
- **Bloods:** (ideally after ROSC, but can draw during arrest if IV already)
    - ABG – pH, lactate, K⁺, glucose, Ca²⁺, base deficit
    - Full blood count, U&Es, troponin
    - Magnesium, phosphate – if refractory VF
- **Imaging:** (post-ROSC)
    - CXR – ET tube position, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema
    - CT head – if coma after ROSC (exclude intracranial catastrophe)
- **Never-miss test:** **Glucose** – bedside finger-prick; treat hypo or hyperglycaemia immediately. Also **pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age – pregnancy changes resuscitation (left lateral tilt, early obstetric team).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Start/continue high-quality CPR (30:2, rate 100–120/min, depth 5–6 cm, allow full chest recoil)
    - Apply defibrillator pads; analyse rhythm every 2 minutes
    - **Shockable (VF/pVT):** Defibrillate 150–200 J (biphasic) → immediately resume CPR for 2 min → check rhythm → repeat shock → give adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO after 3rd shock → amiodarone 300 mg after 3rd shock → repeated defib cycles + adrenaline every 3–5 min + amiodarone 150 mg after 5th shock
    - **Non-shockable (asystole/PEA):** Give adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO as soon as IV access → continue CPR for 2 min → check rhythm → give adrenaline every 3–5 min → treat reversible causes
    - Secure airway – supraglottic device or tracheal intubation (best done by experienced operator); capnography to confirm placement
    - IV/IO access – proximal humerus IO if IV fails after 2 attempts
    - Identify and treat 4Hs/4Ts (e.g. needle decompression for tension pneumothorax, fluid bolus for hypovolaemia, calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** (post-ROSC care)
    - Targeted temperature management (32–36°C) for 24h
    - Controlled oxygenation (SpO₂ 94–98%) – avoid hyperoxia
    - Controlled ventilation – PaCO₂ 4.5–5.5 kPa
    - Haemodynamic optimisation – fluid bolus if hypotensive; consider noradrenaline or dobutamine if shock persists
    - Urgent cardiology review – ECG (within 10 min of ROSC) ± primary PCI if ST-elevation
    - Neuroprognostication after 72h (CT/MRI, SSEPs, clinical exam)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Cause investigation: echo, coronary angiography, EP studies
    - ICD implantation (if ischaemic or non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy with EF ≤35%)
    - CPR training in family members if appropriate
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** (post-ROSC)
    - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s – 30 mL/kg bolus if hypotensive (or guided by dynamic assessment)
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (or weight-based), unless on therapeutic anticoagulation or active bleeding
    - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM until airway extubated/safe swallow confirmed
    - **Observation frequency:** Continuous monitoring (ECG, SpO₂, ETCO₂, BP) in ICU/HDU; if on ward, hourly NEWS2 + cardiac telemetry
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Adrenaline** (epinephrine) 1 mg (10 mL of 1:10,000) IV/IO every 3–5 minutes (do not give more frequently)
    - **Amiodarone** 300 mg IV/IO bolus after 3rd shock (for VF/pVT); then 150 mg after 5th shock
    - **Magnesium sulfate** 2 g IV over 1–2 minutes in torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with prolonged QT) or hypomagnesaemia
    - **Sodium bicarbonate** 50 mmol (50 mL of 8.4%) IV – only if severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1) or hyperkalaemia (or tricyclic overdose) – not routine
- **Common alternatives:**
    - **Lidocaine** (lignocaine) 1–1.5 mg/kg IV – if amiodarone unavailable or refractory torsades
    - **Atropine** – no longer recommended in asystole/PEA (removed from ALS algorithm)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Adrenaline: extreme hypertension, tachyarrhythmia (post-ROSC), but no contraindication during arrest
    - Amiodarone: bradycardia, prolongs QT (monitor post-ROSC); use caution with other QT-prolonging drugs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None required during cardiac arrest. For post-ROSC renal impairment: use contrast with caution if eGFR <30 (but STEMI overrides – [check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Adrenaline – giving repeated doses without checking for ROSC** leads to inappropriate escalation of tachyarrhythmia; give every 3–5 minutes **only** if rhythm remains non-shockable or after shock checks. **Omitting Amiodarone in refractory VF** reduces chance of ROSC.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Rib/sternal fractures, lung contusion, pneumothorax
    - Aspiration pneumonitis
    - External defibrillator burns (reduce risk by ensuring good pad contact)
    - Acute coronary syndrome (from shocks/arrest itself)
    - Anoxic brain injury – immediate or evolving
- **Chronic:**
    - Post-cardiac arrest syndrome (systemic inflammation, myocardial dysfunction, neurological deficit)
    - Cognitive impairment, persistent vegetative state
    - Need for long-term ICD/pacemaker
sections.Prognosis: - Survival to hospital discharge after in-hospital cardiac arrest: ~15–20%; out-of-hospital: ~10%. For shockable rhythms, survival 30–50%. Poor prognostic factors: unwitnessed arrest, asystole, prolonged downtime >30 min, no bystander CPR, lactate >10 mmol/L, absent pupillary reflexes at 72h.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do:** Start chest compressions **before** any rhythm analysis if arrest unwitnessed – minimise pre-shock pause to <5 seconds.
- **Do:** Use capnography to confirm airway placement and monitor CPR quality – rising ETCO₂ is first sign of ROSC.
- **Do:** During ROSC care, target SpO₂ 94–98% and PaCO₂ 4.5–5.5 kPa – hyperoxia and hypocapnia worsen neurological outcome.
- **Do:** Call for ultrasound if you are trained – it can reveal tamponade or severe LV dysfunction during rhythm check pauses.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not:** Interrupt chest compressions for more than 10 seconds except for defibrillation or rhythm analysis.
- **Do not:** Give adrenaline before the first shock in VF/pVT – defibrillation is the priority, adrenaline comes after 3rd shock.
- **Do not:** Abandon CPR early – continue for at least 20 minutes and until reversible causes treated; prolonged resuscitation may still achieve ROSC.
- **Do not:** Forget to check glucose, pregnancy status, and drug levels – these are common reversible causes.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
(Unlikely after full arrest, but for aborted sudden cardiac death or syncope with negative workup)
- **Return if:** Palpitations, syncope, chest pain, or dyspnoea – especially within 48 hours; any new ECG change.
- **Follow-up:** Urgent cardiology clinic within 1 week; arrange 24-hour ECG monitor ± echo; advise driving restrictions per DVLA (cessation for 6 months if ICD not implanted).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call cardiac arrest team / crash team** immediately if patient found unresponsive, not breathing normally – do not wait.
- **After ROSC,** call ICU for admission (all comatose patients post‑cardiac arrest) + cardiology registrar for ECG and ± primary PCI.
- **Specific thresholds:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red) – escalate to nurse in charge + medical registrar
    - Sustained hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite 30 mL/kg fluid – call ICU
    - Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) – call ICU and interventional cardiology
    - Lactate >4 mmol/L after ROSC – consider ICU as high-risk
    - GCS <8 after ROSC – ensure secured airway, call anaesthetics/ICU

rawMarkdown:
## Cardiac Arrest — Adult ALS
**One-liner:** Loss of cardiac output requiring immediate CPR, defibrillation, and ALS algorithm per Resuscitation Council UK.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Unrecognised reversible cause (4Hs/4Ts):** Missed tension pneumothorax → look for tracheal deviation + distended neck veins + absent breath sounds → needle decompression before rhythm analysis.
- **Prolonged “no flow” time:** Every minute without CPR reduces survival by 7–10% → high-quality, uninterrupted chest compressions are the single most effective intervention; defibrillation alone is useless without perfusion.
- **Failure to identify shockable rhythm:** VF/pVT must be shocked immediately – each minute delay reduces defibrillation success by ~10%. Check pad placement and rhythm after pause <10 seconds.

### Presentation
- **Unresponsive** (no response to voice or pain)
- **Not breathing normally** (agonal gasps count as not normal)
- **No signs of life** (no movement, no coughing, no pulse – but pulse check is unreliable; trust clinical gestalt)
- **Rhythm on monitor:** VF, pulseless VT, asystole, or PEA (any organised rhythm with no pulse)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Shockable: VF / pVT** → immediate defibrillation; treat ischaemia, electrolyte disturbance, drugs (e.g. cocaine, tricyclics)
- **Non-shockable: Asystole / PEA** → adrenaline early; investigate 4Hs/4Ts
    - Hypoxia
    - Hypovolaemia (haemorrhage)
    - Hypo-/hyperkalaemia (check ABG)
    - Hypothermia
    - Tension pneumothorax
    - Tamponade (cardiac)
    - Toxins (overdose)
    - Thrombosis (pulmonary / coronary)
- **Splitter:** Rhythm on monitor – shockable or non-shockable bifurcates the entire management pathway.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - Defibrillator pads + monitor – confirm rhythm
    - Capnography (ETCO₂) – rising waveform marks ROSC; <10 mmHg indicates poor CPR quality
    - Ultrasound (if available and you are trained) – assess for tamponade, pneumothorax, LV contractility; use during <10-second pauses **never** interrupt chest compressions for scanning
- **Bloods:** (ideally after ROSC, but can draw during arrest if IV already)
    - ABG – pH, lactate, K⁺, glucose, Ca²⁺, base deficit
    - Full blood count, U&Es, troponin
    - Magnesium, phosphate – if refractory VF
- **Imaging:** (post-ROSC)
    - CXR – ET tube position, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema
    - CT head – if coma after ROSC (exclude intracranial catastrophe)
- **Never-miss test:** **Glucose** – bedside finger-prick; treat hypo or hyperglycaemia immediately. Also **pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age – pregnancy changes resuscitation (left lateral tilt, early obstetric team).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Start/continue high-quality CPR (30:2, rate 100–120/min, depth 5–6 cm, allow full chest recoil)
    - Apply defibrillator pads; analyse rhythm every 2 minutes
    - **Shockable (VF/pVT):** Defibrillate 150–200 J (biphasic) → immediately resume CPR for 2 min → check rhythm → repeat shock → give adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO after 3rd shock → amiodarone 300 mg after 3rd shock → repeated defib cycles + adrenaline every 3–5 min + amiodarone 150 mg after 5th shock
    - **Non-shockable (asystole/PEA):** Give adrenaline 1 mg IV/IO as soon as IV access → continue CPR for 2 min → check rhythm → give adrenaline every 3–5 min → treat reversible causes
    - Secure airway – supraglottic device or tracheal intubation (best done by experienced operator); capnography to confirm placement
    - IV/IO access – proximal humerus IO if IV fails after 2 attempts
    - Identify and treat 4Hs/4Ts (e.g. needle decompression for tension pneumothorax, fluid bolus for hypovolaemia, calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** (post-ROSC care)
    - Targeted temperature management (32–36°C) for 24h
    - Controlled oxygenation (SpO₂ 94–98%) – avoid hyperoxia
    - Controlled ventilation – PaCO₂ 4.5–5.5 kPa
    - Haemodynamic optimisation – fluid bolus if hypotensive; consider noradrenaline or dobutamine if shock persists
    - Urgent cardiology review – ECG (within 10 min of ROSC) ± primary PCI if ST-elevation
    - Neuroprognostication after 72h (CT/MRI, SSEPs, clinical exam)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Cause investigation: echo, coronary angiography, EP studies
    - ICD implantation (if ischaemic or non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy with EF ≤35%)
    - CPR training in family members if appropriate
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** (post-ROSC)
    - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s – 30 mL/kg bolus if hypotensive (or guided by dynamic assessment)
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (or weight-based), unless on therapeutic anticoagulation or active bleeding
    - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM until airway extubated/safe swallow confirmed
    - **Observation frequency:** Continuous monitoring (ECG, SpO₂, ETCO₂, BP) in ICU/HDU; if on ward, hourly NEWS2 + cardiac telemetry

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Adrenaline** (epinephrine) 1 mg (10 mL of 1:10,000) IV/IO every 3–5 minutes (do not give more frequently)
    - **Amiodarone** 300 mg IV/IO bolus after 3rd shock (for VF/pVT); then 150 mg after 5th shock
    - **Magnesium sulfate** 2 g IV over 1–2 minutes in torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with prolonged QT) or hypomagnesaemia
    - **Sodium bicarbonate** 50 mmol (50 mL of 8.4%) IV – only if severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1) or hyperkalaemia (or tricyclic overdose) – not routine
- **Common alternatives:**
    - **Lidocaine** (lignocaine) 1–1.5 mg/kg IV – if amiodarone unavailable or refractory torsades
    - **Atropine** – no longer recommended in asystole/PEA (removed from ALS algorithm)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Adrenaline: extreme hypertension, tachyarrhythmia (post-ROSC), but no contraindication during arrest
    - Amiodarone: bradycardia, prolongs QT (monitor post-ROSC); use caution with other QT-prolonging drugs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None required during cardiac arrest. For post-ROSC renal impairment: use contrast with caution if eGFR <30 (but STEMI overrides – [check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Adrenaline – giving repeated doses without checking for ROSC** leads to inappropriate escalation of tachyarrhythmia; give every 3–5 minutes **only** if rhythm remains non-shockable or after shock checks. **Omitting Amiodarone in refractory VF** reduces chance of ROSC.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Rib/sternal fractures, lung contusion, pneumothorax
    - Aspiration pneumonitis
    - External defibrillator burns (reduce risk by ensuring good pad contact)
    - Acute coronary syndrome (from shocks/arrest itself)
    - Anoxic brain injury – immediate or evolving
- **Chronic:**
    - Post-cardiac arrest syndrome (systemic inflammation, myocardial dysfunction, neurological deficit)
    - Cognitive impairment, persistent vegetative state
    - Need for long-term ICD/pacemaker

### Prognosis
- Survival to hospital discharge after in-hospital cardiac arrest: ~15–20%; out-of-hospital: ~10%. For shockable rhythms, survival 30–50%. Poor prognostic factors: unwitnessed arrest, asystole, prolonged downtime >30 min, no bystander CPR, lactate >10 mmol/L, absent pupillary reflexes at 72h.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do:** Start chest compressions **before** any rhythm analysis if arrest unwitnessed – minimise pre-shock pause to <5 seconds.
- **Do:** Use capnography to confirm airway placement and monitor CPR quality – rising ETCO₂ is first sign of ROSC.
- **Do:** During ROSC care, target SpO₂ 94–98% and PaCO₂ 4.5–5.5 kPa – hyperoxia and hypocapnia worsen neurological outcome.
- **Do:** Call for ultrasound if you are trained – it can reveal tamponade or severe LV dysfunction during rhythm check pauses.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not:** Interrupt chest compressions for more than 10 seconds except for defibrillation or rhythm analysis.
- **Do not:** Give adrenaline before the first shock in VF/pVT – defibrillation is the priority, adrenaline comes after 3rd shock.
- **Do not:** Abandon CPR early – continue for at least 20 minutes and until reversible causes treated; prolonged resuscitation may still achieve ROSC.
- **Do not:** Forget to check glucose, pregnancy status, and drug levels – these are common reversible causes.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
(Unlikely after full arrest, but for aborted sudden cardiac death or syncope with negative workup)
- **Return if:** Palpitations, syncope, chest pain, or dyspnoea – especially within 48 hours; any new ECG change.
- **Follow-up:** Urgent cardiology clinic within 1 week; arrange 24-hour ECG monitor ± echo; advise driving restrictions per DVLA (cessation for 6 months if ICD not implanted).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call cardiac arrest team / crash team** immediately if patient found unresponsive, not breathing normally – do not wait.
- **After ROSC,** call ICU for admission (all comatose patients post‑cardiac arrest) + cardiology registrar for ECG and ± primary PCI.
- **Specific thresholds:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red) – escalate to nurse in charge + medical registrar
    - Sustained hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite 30 mL/kg fluid – call ICU
    - Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) – call ICU and interventional cardiology
    - Lactate >4 mmol/L after ROSC – consider ICU as high-risk
    - GCS <8 after ROSC – ensure secured airway, call anaesthetics/ICU
